data.title: Trump approves tiny ‘cars of the very near future’
data.description:
President Trump said Friday he had granted approval for “tiny cars” to be built in the United States, a move that would expand the availability of similar smaller vehicles that are popular in Asia.

“I have just approved TINY CARS to be built in America. Manufacturers have long wanted to do this, just like they are so successfully built in other countries,” Trump posted on Truth Social. “They can be propelled by gasoline, electric, or hybrid.

“These cars of the very near future are inexpensive, safe, fuel efficient and, quite simply, AMAZING!!! START BUILDING THEM NOW!”

Trump had earlier in the week expressed admiration for Kei cars, small vehicles that are common in Japan and elsewhere in Asia that are sold at a lower cost and are tailored to narrower roadways.

“They have a very small car. It’s sort of like the Beetle used to be with the Volkswagen,” Trump said Wednesday. “They’re very small, they’re really cute. And I said, ‘How would that do in this country?’ And everyone seems to think good, but you’re not allowed to build them.”

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy told CNBC that tiny cars in the U.S. would likely work best in urban settings and could provide a cheaper alternative for some consumers.

Trump earlier this week also announced his administration was proposing to roll back vehicle mileage standards put in place under the Biden administration. The Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE) standards in question, set by the Transportation Department, set minimum miles per gallon that automakers’ fleets have to meet.

data.title: Who has the best coverage? New FCC data compares Verizon, T-Mobile, AT&T in every state
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Cell providers all claim to have the best coverage, fastest speeds or most reliability in their ad campaigns. But who really comes out on top?

Data released last month by the Federal Communications Commission allows you to put providers head-to-head and see who has the best data coverage where you live.

If you’re looking nationally, it’s a pretty tight race for the top spot. When comparing the three biggest providers – Verizon, T-Mobile and AT&T – on their 4G LTE coverage, Verizon lands in first place by a hair. It has 60% of the country covered, AT&T has 57% and T-Mobile has 45%.

When you compare 5G coverage, however, the rankings shift. T-Mobile comes in first place for 5G data coverage, AT&T is second and Verizon is third.

The FCC tracks two speeds of 5G coverage: 7/1 Mbps and the faster 35/3 Mbps. The maps below show each cell provider’s coverage for the fastest 5G speeds when someone is outdoors and standing still. The coverage for people trying to use data in a moving car drops substantially across the board.

How to check coverage near you

The FCC National Broadband Map allows you to type in your address and dive into the connectivity data at your address. You can compare speeds for when you’re outdoors and stationary versus in a moving vehicle, 4G versus 5G, and check out the coverage from smaller cell companies.

To make it easier, we’ve created three shortcuts for you: a map that compares 4G coverage, a map that compares the lower tier of 5G coverage, and a map that compares the highest tier of 5G coverage.

You can click one of the links above to open a map, zoom in to where you live, and toggle between the three major providers using the settings on the right side.

You can also start from square one by putting your address in here, clicking the Mobile Broadband option on the right-hand side, and the Provider Detail tab at the top. From there, you can add providers to compare.

With the update released in November, the FCC map now shows data for 2025. The FCC refreshes the map regularly to reflect changes in coverage.

data.title: Trump can’t save Hegseth now — no one can
data.description:
There’s a lot of discussion as to whether the U.S. Navy’s “double tap” strike of Sept. 2 — the latter hit killing two survivors clinging to the wreckage of an alleged drug boat — constitutes a war crime. There’s also a debate about whether it was Secretary of War Pete Hegseth himself that ordered the Navy to “kill everybody.”

Let’s assume that Hegseth gave the order and ask a more important question — one that has serious ramifications for every member of the U.S. military, from the lowliest private to the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

Can President Trump pardon Hegseth? 

Trump has been very free with his use of the pardon power, even for convicted drug traffickers. There’s little doubt he would pardon an underling like Hegseth, especially if, in his view, Hegseth’s only crime was carrying out Trump’s own policies too zealously. There’s also no question that the president has broad pardon powers, extending to all “offences against the United States, except in cases of impeachment.”

But war crimes are different from ordinary crimes. The statute that authorizes their prosecution is very broad. Anyone who has committed a war crime against anyone, anywhere can be prosecuted for that crime if they are present in the United States. The penalty for committing a war crime can range from a fine all the way up to the death penalty if the victim of the war crime is killed.

War crimes themselves are not defined by U.S. law, but, rather, by international law. The War Crimes Act that authorizes the Department of Justice to prosecute war crimes using international law defines them as, among other things, a “grave breach of the Geneva Convention” and violations of Articles 23, 25, 27, or 28 of the Annex to the 1907 Hague Convention. These conventions specifically prohibit issuing “no quarter” orders and intentionally killing “those placed out of combat by sickness, wounds, detention, or any other cause” — both of which Hegseth is alleged to have done.

So while Hegseth could be pardoned for committing murder in violation of federal law, he cannot be pardoned for committing a war crime, any more than an American president could have pardoned the Nuremberg defendants. Violations of the Geneva convention are not “offenses against the United States,” but offenses against international law.  

The statute under which Hegseth would be prosecuted, 18 USC 2441, simply allows the U.S. government to prosecute violations of the international laws of war in American courts, even when the United States has no connection to the violation itself. The first case brought under the War Crimes Act involved Russian soldiers fighting in Ukraine. 

It is true that presidents, including Trump, have pardoned past acts that would constitute war crimes. But the perpetrators were charged with violating either the Uniform Code of Military Justice or federal criminal law — in other words, offenses against the United States, rather than the Geneva Convention or the Hague Convention, which are offenses against international law. The War Crimes Act has only been invoked once since it became law in 1996. 

Of course, Trump could forbid the Department of Justice from prosecuting Hegseth while he is president. But since he cannot pardon violations of international law, he could not prevent a future administration from prosecuting Hegseth, just as he could not forbid another country from exercising its own jurisdiction to prosecute war crimes. And in Hegseth’s case, assuming his orders caused the intentional killing of a protected person, there is no statute of limitations. 

This has profound implications for the military and how it conducts itself. There are many reasons, both ethical and legal, for refusing to commit war crimes. If nothing else, war crimes are antithetical to the American military tradition. But there is also a very practical reason. If you commit an act that violates the laws of war — especially one that results in an intentional killing — sooner or later, you can be held accountable, and the president cannot save you.   

Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) and his compatriots are right. Illegal orders, especially those that will result in the unlawful killing of noncombatants, can and must be refused. If Hegseth can be prosecuted under the laws of war for giving the order, so can the soldier who pulled the trigger.

Chris Truax is a charter member of the Society for the Rule of Law and an appellate attorney.

data.title: Court blocks Trump administration effort to halt school mental health worker grants
data.description:
A federal appeals court on Thursday halted an effort by the Trump administration to block grants to hire more school mental health workers. 

The 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals denied the administration’s appeal of a district court decision to restore grants given by the Biden administration to hire more counselors and social workers, particularly in low-income and rural schools. 

The appeals court ruled the Trump administration has not shown a strong likelihood of success and noted the district court is likely to reach a final decision soon. 

The Hill has reached out to the Education Department for comment. 

The Trump administration is fighting these grants because it opposes some DEI aspects in the programs and is trying to cut off funding after this month. 

The termination of grants was challenged by 16 Democratic-led states, with the district court ruling restoring the grants only applying to those states. 

The grants were approved in 2022 as concerns rose over the mental health impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on students. 

This isn’t the first time Democratic states and lawmakers have banded together to fight back against moves at the Education Department. 

Back in March, some states challenged the termination of more than 1,300 department employees. Senate Democrats on Thursday sent a letter to the department calling its recent announcement to move some programs to other federal programs “illegal.”

data.title: Meghan McCain rips weight remarks on Tucker Carlson podcast
data.description:
Conservative pundit Meghan McCain blasted commentator Tucker Carlson after a guest on his podcast and online show this week made a disparaging remark about her weight.

Carlson hosted Milo Yiannopoulos, another right-wing pundit, for an episode released Wednesday during which the two talked extensively and critically about homosexuality.

Yiannopoulos, at one point during the conversation, mentioned the late Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.), Meghan’s father, and called her “fat.”

“Yes, I am very fat, I’m almost 9 months pregnant with my third child. I am also very happy,” McCain wrote on the social platform X. “I don’t care how many times Tucker talks about being a Christian, that is not Christian behavior to engage in such a conversation on one of the largest podcasts in the world.”

McCain pointed to the episode as “a classic example of why the right has such a problem with women voters of my generation.”

“Don’t feel bad for me, people have been calling me fat since I started in media at age 22. It is old, lazy, tired,” she continued. “I don’t make my living off of ugliness like that and truly can’t fathom what life is like for anyone who does. It must be very dark.”

McCain has been sharply critical of Carlson in the past, and is a former host of ABC’s “The View,” a gig she vacated in 2021 to start her own media venture and focus on her family, she said at the time.

data.title: Warner: ‘It’s a little rich’ DOJ is saying they make America safer
data.description:
Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) on Thursday said “it’s a little rich” that the Department of Justice (DOJ) claimed they are making the country safer despite President Trump’s pardon of rioters tied to the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the Capitol.

Speaking on MS NOW’s “Katy Tur Reports,” Warner praised the DOJ’s investigation that led to the arrest of a Virginia man in connection with pipe bombs planted outside the Democratic and Republican national committee offices on Jan. 5, 2021. The senator called the arrest “good news.”

But, he added, “It’s a little rich that they’re saying America’s safer.

“How much earlier could we have caught this guy if resources hadn’t been diverted? And I hope it would also remind folks that on Jan. 6 — I was here at the Capitol on Jan. 6. It was an ugly, awful day,” the Virginia Democrat continued. “And this administration and this president basically pardoned all the perpetrators.”

The senator added that the Trump administration’s “picking and choosing of facts” also makes him “little bit crazy.”

On Thursday, the FBI announced that 30-year-old Brian Cole, of Woodbridge, Va., was charged with transporting an explosive device in interstate commerce and attempted malicious destruction by means of explosive materials. Investigators for years could only identify the suspect as a man with a hoodie and a face mask wearing Nike Air Max Sneakers, with an estimated height of 5’6.”

Forensic evidence was found on various pieces used to build the bombs, according to the DOJ.

U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia Jeanine Pirro said Thursday that 233,000 end caps were sold like the ones used in the bomb allegedly made by Cole. According to charging documents, Cole bought 12 black end caps and two galvanized end caps from four Home Depots in Northern Virginia between October 2019 and November 2020.

Cell phone data from the suspect’s phone also showed he was connected to towers near the RNC and DNC buildings on Jan. 5, per the complaint. His car was also seen driving past the South Capitol Street exit that day, less than half a mile from where the person suspected of placing the bombs was seen walking shortly after.

Upon President Trump’s return to the White House in January, he signed an executive order pardoning those found guilty of assaulting police officers during the Capitol insurrection. He pardoned almost all of the roughly 1,600 people charged in connection with the incident and commuted the sentences of about a dozen others who did not receive pardons — including extremist group leaders and those charged for violence.

Trump had also ordered the DOJ to throw out any pending indictments against Jan. 6 defendants whose cases had not been adjudicated.

One week signing the order, Senate Democrats filed a resolution to condemn the pardons. Sen. Patty Murray (D-Wash.) called the clemencies, “a wholesale endorsement of political violence — as long as it serves Donald Trump.”

“Affirming that U.S. Senators condemn unconditional pardons for people who were found guilty of violently assaulting Capitol Police officers should be the easiest thing in the world,” she said at the time.

data.title: Noem says Trump weighing expanding travel ban to more than 30 countries
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said the Trump administration is planning to expand its travel ban to more than 30 countries in the wake of a deadly shooting against National Guardsmen.

“I won’t be specific on the number, but it’s over 30, and the President is continuing to evaluate countries,” Noem said Thursday evening in an appearance on Fox News’s “The Ingraham Angle.”

“Listen, if they don’t have a stable government there, if they don’t have a country that can sustain itself and tell us who those individuals are and help us vet them, why should we allow people from that country to come here to the United States,” she told host Laura Ingraham.

The Trump administration has already banned travel from 19 different countries it has deemed a security and public safety threat: Afghanistan, Burundi, Chad, Cuba, Republic of Congo, Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Haiti, Iran, Laos, Libya, Myanmar, Sierra Leone, Somalia, Sudan, Togo, Turkmenistan, Venezuela and Yemen.

Noem would not confirm the number posed by Ingraham, who asked whether the list would rise to 32 countries.

President Trump has taken sweeping immigration actions in the wake of the National Guard shooting in Washington late last month, after he pledged to “permanently pause migration from all Third World Countries.”

The administration has paused all pending asylum applications for those from across the globe seeking refuge in the U.S. And for those from the 19 countries already covered by the travel ban, Trump has said he would pause review of their immigration cases and threatened to revoke their green cards.

He also suggested the U.S. would not issue any visas to citizens of Afghanistan — the home country of alleged shooter Rahmanullah Lakanwal — in a move that effectively shuts off the pathway for those who assisted U.S. military efforts to leave the country under the Biden administration.

data.title: Read: White House National Security Strategy
data.description:
The White House paints European allies as weak in a new national security strategy released early Friday that outlines plans to reassert America’s dominance in the Western Hemisphere.

The administration targets European nations over migration and free speech policies, suggesting they face the “prospect of civilizational erasure” and raising doubts about their long-term reliability as partners.

Read the document here:

The Associated Press contributed

data.title: To build ships faster, the Navy needs to change how it designs them
data.description:
Two days before Thanksgiving, Navy Secretary John Phelan announced the cancellation of the $22 billion 20 ship Constellation multi-mission frigate program. The ship was meant to be capable of conducting anti-air, anti-surface, anti-submarine and electromagnetic warfare both in littoral areas and on the high seas.

The Navy had already spent about $2 billion of the $7.6 billion that the Congress had appropriated for the first six ships of the class. Phelan stated that he would not halt the construction that had already begun on the first two ships, but was terminating the construction of the remaining four ships. As Phelan put it, “a key factor in this decision is the need to grow the fleet faster to meet tomorrow’s threats.”

Phelan is right. The first ship of the class was meant to be at sea by next year; delivery is now estimated to be three years later, in 2029. And that presupposes that there will be no further delays. 

That assumption may still be optimistic. The delays were due mainly to design issues. At the program’s outset, the Navy asserted that “‘basic and functional designs’ were 88 percent complete.” But the Navy’s statement masked the fact that the ship’s overall design was far from finished.

The Constellation was meant to be a modified version of the Italian-French FREMM frigate. Initially, the Navy claimed that it would incorporate about 85 percent of the European design into its new frigate. The Navy chose to enlarge and reconfigure the ship, however. As a result, the Constellation had only 15 percent commonality with its European “parent design.”

The Navy seemed to be uncertain when exactly the design would actually be completed. In August 2022, when, as a Congressional Research Service report has noted, “the Navy greenlit [the shipbuilder] to begin building the first frigate,” the Navy announced that “the detail design was just over 80 percent complete.” Yet two years later, the Navy said the design was under 80 percent complete. Clearly, not only was the Navy far from completing its design, it seemed to be revisiting designs it had already approved.

All the while, construction was underway. The GAO pointed out that “the Navy’s decision to approve the shipbuilder to begin construction with an incomplete design is inconsistent with leading ship design practices.” Not surprisingly, the design changes led to schedule delays. Schedule delays led to price increases. The program was simply no longer viable.

The sad tale of the Constellation’s demise is hardly unique. In the late 1990s, the Navy began work on a destroyer it called the DD-21. In 2005 the program was reclassified as the Zumwalt class (DDG-1000). The Navy intended to construct 32 ships of this class of large land attack destroyers that was meant to draw upon the latest in stealth technology. Only three years later, the Navy announced that it would only procure two ships, which were by then almost completed. In 2009 Congress, funded a third ship, also already under construction.

Poor design planning killed the ship. The original design tried to incorporate too many new and untested technologies simultaneously. Moreover, the ship had been designed as a land attack ship, but the Navy soon concluded that it instead needed to augment its sea-based anti-ballistic missile capability. The ship could not support this mission without a major redesign. In the meantime, the cost of the ship kept rising. By the time the program was effectively terminated, it had cost $22 billion.

Design issues also undermined the Littoral Combat Ship program. In 2002, when the Navy first briefed me on a replacement to the increasingly obsolescent Perry class frigate, it appeared to envisage a smaller, multi-mission corvette, which it named the Littoral Combat Ship, that would displace approximately 2,000 tons. But design issues and the Navy’s seeming penchant for larger ships resulted in two variants — both fifty percent larger, with displacements in excess of 3,100 tons. The design changes again led to technical failures, schedule delays, outsized cost increases and operational breakdowns. 

The result? A program meant to yield more than 50 ships dropped to 23, with ships retired from the fleet well before their operating lifetimes were over. One was retired after only three years of service.

Phelan has made it clear that “it is time to deliver the ship our warfighters need at a pace that matches the threat environment, not the comfort level of the bureaucracy.” The Navy can only meet the secretary’s demand if it fundamentally changes its approach to ship design. 

It is time the Navy adhered to basic principles, which require that a ship’s design truly be near completion before work on it commences. Otherwise, it will continue to produce ships that are overpriced and behind schedule, even as China expands and continues to modernize what is already the world’s most numerous, if not its most capable, fighting fleet.

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: Speaker Johnson faces a Republican women’s revolt
data.description:
He’s got 99 problems, and one keeps getting worse: House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) is taking fire from several high-profile Republican women, and the list keeps growing.

Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-Fla.), is miffed that Johnson won’t schedule a vote to ban congressional stock trading. Rep. Elise Stefanik (R-N.Y.) is furious that a pet provision didn’t make it into the defense bill. She claims that Johnson lied to her and got “rolled” by Democrats. 

Then there’s Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), a candidate for governor who was reportedly so fed up that she contemplated bailing out of the House before her term is up — an allegation she denies despite still insisting that, yes, she’s frustrated.

And Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-GA), who is actually resigning in January, has been calling for Johnson’s head for years.

Greene insists there’s a “night and day” difference between how Johnson treats Republican women compared to his predecessor, former Rep. Kevin McCarthy (R-Calif.). This raises the question: how exactly did McCarthy treat women in his caucus?

According to McCarthy, his “formula for success” was to recruit more women, more minorities, and more veterans so the conference “would look more like America.”

That noble sentiment, if you squint even a little, sounds suspiciously like diversity, equity, and inclusion or DEI. 

It is, perhaps, ironic that Republican women are suddenly expressing shock — shock! — that a party built on Trump’s macho, politically incorrect swagger might occasionally behave in ways consistent with Trump’s macho, politically incorrect swagger.

Still, there’s a clear attempt to pin this unchivalrous milieu on Johnson personally. As some Republican women suggested to The New York Times, the speaker — an evangelical fond of lecturing about “distinct roles” for men and women — struggles when confronted with women who dare to wield political power.

NBC News heard similar complaints. One Republican said Johnson is “undercutting” some of his own women members, predicting there would be fewer of them back next year. Another was more blunt: “We aren’t taken seriously. You have women who are very accomplished, very successful, who have earned the merit, who aren’t given the time of the day.”

To be fair, Johnson’s job is a tough one. With a majority so slim it take a magnifying glass to see, he has less room to maneuver than a hamster in a shoebox. 

But Johnson’s 99 problems don’t end there. Everyone can see the Democrats approaching the House majority like a slow-moving but unstoppable freight train. Retiring Republicans are like the people who read the weather report and then evacuate while the roads are still open.

And then there’s Trump, who has never met a Republican headache he didn’t create. He pardons a drug kingpin while boasting about killing drug traffickers at sea, and you’re still not allowed to criticize him.

Even Greene, who managed to infuriate Trump over the Epstein files, continues to praise him between her tirades. Loyalty, after all, is a one-way street.

So where do disgruntled members vent their frustration? When compared with Trump, Johnson is politically harmless and physically unthreatening. He is perpetually the whipping boy for a good public thrashing.

He is also trapped in a job that lost most of its sticks and arrows sometime around the Reagan administration.

Meanwhile, Trump is off playing culture-war emperor, firing off Truth Social ramblings and executive orders. Legislating? Please. That’s for suckers. So if you’re a member of Congress who actually came to Washington to do something, you are destined to be disappointed.

This raises the question: Are women more interested in actually getting things done?

Greene, at least, seems to hint that this is true. Last month — subtle as always — she declared there are “a lot of weak Republican men” who “try to marginalize the strong Republican women.”

On The View, she doubled down: “When I talk about weak Republican men … I am talking about House and Senate leadership. They just aren’t getting our agenda done.”

Imagine the reaction on Fox News if someone like AOC were to make the same sweeping claim about men. Trumpworld would spontaneously combust.

Still, Greene’s broader frustration is understandable, even if it’s a gender-neutral problem.

Rank-and-file members have watched their power evaporate for decades, and Trump’s imperial presidency has turned Congress into the world’s most resentful bystander.

Who needs them to appropriate money? Corporate donors will pick up the slack. Who needs them to not spend money? Rescissions will take care of that. Declare war? Please. There are narco-terrorists to bomb.

So is Johnson a retrograde misogynist, or just an unlucky patsy occupying an obsolete job while running the least functional workplace in Washington? Either way, the ending is the same.

Whether he’s toppled by an internal coup or swept away by November’s likely Democratic wave, Johnson’s grip on the gavel appears to be loosening. 

Matt K. Lewis is a columnist, podcaster, and author of the books “Too Dumb to Fail” and “Filthy Rich Politicians.”
